original_abstract: Publicly available scientific resources help establish evaluation standards, provide a platform for teaching and improve reproducibility. Version 4 of the Insight ToolKit (ITK(4)) seeks to establish new standards in publicly available image registration methodology. ITK(4) makes several advances in comparison to previous versions of ITK. ITK(4) supports both multivariate images and objective functions; it also unifies high-dimensional (deformation field) and low-dimensional (affine) transformations with metrics that are reusable across transform types and with composite transforms that allow arbitrary series of geometric mappings to be chained together seamlessly. Metrics and optimizers take advantage of multi-core resources, when available. Furthermore, ITK(4) reduces the parameter optimization burden via principled heuristics that automatically set scaling across disparate parameter types (rotations vs. translations). A related approach also constrains steps sizes for gradient-based optimizers. The result is that tuning for different metrics and/or image pairs is rarely necessary allowing the researcher to more easily focus on design/comparison of registration strategies. In total, the ITK(4) contribution is intended as a structure to support reproducible research practices, will provide a more extensive foundation against which to evaluate new work in image registration and also enable application level programmers a broad suite of tools on which to build. Finally, we contextualize this work with a reference registration evaluation study with application to pediatric brain labeling.
humanized_abstract: Publicly available scientific resources should define evaluation criteria, provide materials for instruction, and enable experimental results to be reproduced by others. Our goal with version 4 of the Insight ToolKit (ITK(4))) is to set a new standard for publicly available image registration techniques. A number of improvements have been made relative to previous releases of ITK: The most significant improvement is that it is now possible to work with multivariate images and objective functions. ITK(4) unifies high-dimensional (deformation fields) and low-dimensional (affine parameters) transformations and corresponding metrics in such a way that the metrics can be easily re-used across both transform domains, and composite transforms allow an arbitrary sequence of transformations to be concatenated, such that geometric transformation composition becomes a trivial operation. Several of the metrics and optimizers in ITK have been threaded to utilize multiple CPU cores when available. ITK(4) reduces the amount of user interaction needed to obtain good results for registration by introducing several design principles to set meaningful scales for differing parameters in an automated fashion (e.g. rotations have different scales than translations). Stopping step size values are also automatically introduced in gradient-based optimizers to assist in convergence to an optimum. With a few exceptions, user choice (e.g. which metric, which pair of images, etc.) does not affect the basic ability of a registration to converge to meaningful results, and the application programmer can instead focus on comparison and design of registration techniques. The overall purpose of the ITK(4) advances is to establish a base of common tools that can be used to support the principles of reproducible research in the medical image analysis community, provide a larger benchmark against which new techniques can be validated, and allow application-level users of medical image processing algorithms to work with a broad base of versatile tools. To demonstrate the potential impact of this release, we present an image registration evaluation study of 14 publicly available atlases of labeled pediatric brains.
